chunks.text:
THE FRAMING QUESTION
As value creation shifts from labor to capital,
how must the U.S. tax system evolve?

WHY IT MATTERS
• Military & defense funding
• Infrastructure investment
• Social safety net programs
• America's global competitiveness

GANS CONNECTION
AI improves prediction quality: Gans Ch. 3 frames AI as a
prediction machine that makes decisions cheaper and
better. This essay asks: How can the government adapt
to capture that value?

THE FISCAL MATH: LABOR → CAPITAL
10–15¢
lost per $1 shifted
from labor to capital

Effective Tax Rate Gap
LABOR INCOME: ~30% effective rate
CAPITAL INCOME: ~15–20% rate → Half the revenue

CONCRETE EXAMPLE: THE RADIOLOGIST
Human Radiologist earns $500K/yr → generates ~$200K in federal + state tax revenue
AI replaces them → diagnostic value is preserved (or even improves)
But if the AI company reinvests profits rather than paying wages or dividends:
Tax revenue drops to a fraction of the original amount

PHASE 1
Twilight of Labor
AI handles tasks across the economy — customer
service, legal research, medical diagnostics

Labor income tax revenue declines as a share of GDP

Key tension:
Economy grows, but government's ability to fund
public goods shrinks
Gans Ch. 4: AI is a substitute for rules-based decisions. Firms
moving from rules to decisions reduce predictable employment
patterns.

AI-Dominated Economy
AI companies reinvest most profits to expand
capabilities rather than pay wages or dividends

Both labor and consumption tax bases erode

The "forest" metaphor:
Economy keeps growing but value is hard to "harvest"
through traditional tax channels
Labor share fell from 68% (2000) to 58% (2025), costing
hundreds of billions in annual federal revenue. AI threatens to
accelerate this (i.e. increase layoffs).

PHASE 1 SOLUTION: EMBRACE CONSUMPTION TAXATION
Shift from taxing what people earn → taxing what people spend

VALUE-ADDED TAX (VAT)
Used by virtually every high-income country except the
U.S.

Self-enforcing: businesses deduct VAT paid to suppliers,
creating a paper trail that makes evasion difficult

Harder to shift offshore than income taxes which protects
American competitiveness

THE KEY PRINCIPLE
Tax AI services, not AI infrastructure

This avoids tax pyramiding: taxing value multiple times
through the production chain.
Incentivizes productive capital investment.

Analogy : Tax the law firm's client invoice not the electricity
or the computer the lawyer used.

Used by virtually every high-income country except the
U.S.

Self-enforcing: businesses deduct VAT paid to suppliers,
creating a paper trail that makes evasion difficult

Harder to shift offshore than income taxes which protects
American competitiveness

THE KEY PRINCIPLE
Tax AI services, not AI infrastructure

Why? Avoids tax pyramiding: taxing value multiple times
through the production chain. Incentivizes productive capital
investment.

Analogy : Tax the law firm's client invoice — not the
electricity or the computer the lawyer used.

API Usage TAX ✓
Consumer uses AI assistant (API usage) EXEMPT ✗
Business uses AI for internal ops
Robotics TAX ✓
Household robot providing cleaning services to
homeowners EXEMPT ✗
Company buying robots for its fleet

Generative AI TAX ✓
AI-generated text/images for end consumers EXEMPT ✗
GPU ownership / server farm infrastructure

API Usage EXEMPT ✗
Business use AI for internal ops
Robotics EXEMPT ✗
Company buying robots for its fleet

Generative AI EXEMPT ✗
GPU ownership / server farm infrastructure CRITIQUE
Companies incentive to move from L to K is maximized

CRITIQUE
If you exempt server farms from taxation, you're
exempting the single largest capital investment AI
companies make.

FIXED-FACTOR TAXATION
Tax scarce resources that can't flee to tax havens: land, spectrum rights, orbital satellite slots, critical natural resources. Can
generate 5–10% of GDP in revenue (estimation by Korinek).

DIFFERENTIAL COMMODITY TAXES
Ramsey (1927): higher taxes on goods harder to evade or produce at home. Restaurant meals > groceries. Yacht maintenance >
basic transport. Entertainment > basic utilities.

CAPITAL TAXATION (LAST RESORT)
Keep as low as possible since it discourages investment, and only use if consumption taxes alone prove insufficient.

PHASE 2: THE OPTIMAL HARVESTING FRAMEWORK
"Just as a forest manager must decide how much timber to harvest each year to balance current needs against future growth,
society must determine the optimal rate at which to tax AI capital."

THE KEY INSIGHT
Optimal tax rate on AI capital ≈ society's discount rate

Example: if society discounts future consumption at 4%, the optimal
annual AI capital tax is 4%

Patient society (values future generations > ) → tax lightly, let AI
compound
Impatient society (values current needs > ) → tax more, harvest
value now

Tax base metrics:
• Computing resources deployed
• Robotic capital deployed
• Total capital accumulation

Requires international coordination to prevent AI
companies from relocating to tax havens

STRUCTURAL ALTERNATIVES TO TAXATION
The authors propose three non-tax mechanisms to capture AI's value for the public:

1PUBLIC CO-INVESTMENT
Government provides data, infrastructure, or research support and takes equity stakes in return. Scales automatically with AI
company success.
Ex: Norway's sovereign wealth fund from oil revenues

2INFRASTRUCTURE-FOR-ACCESS DEALS
Instead of taxing, governments negotiate revenue-sharing agreements in exchange for providing valuable inputs: govt data, research
facilities, test environments, regulatory clarity.
Ex.: spectrum auctions, mineral rights, toll road franchises.

3AI DEVELOPMENT COMPACTS
Voluntary agreements where AI companies commit a % of gains to public benefit in exchange for regulatory clarity and
infrastructure support.

chunks.section: Ch. 3: AI as a Prediction Machine
chunks.text:
Ch. 3: AI as a Prediction Machine:
AI lowers the cost of prediction. This generates lots of
value but Gans shows the value is owned to whoever
controls the AI, not the workers it replaced.

chunks.section: Ch. 4: Moving from Rules to Decisions
chunks.text:
Ch. 4: Moving from Rules to Decisions:
When AI enables firms to make decisions instead of
following rules, it reduces the need for judgment-based
labor roles. This directly explains why labor income and
therefore labor taxes declines.

AI is imperfect prediction:
Gans Ch. 3 emphasizes that AI signals are never perfect
they have false positive and false negative rates. Think
of a doctor reading an X-ray vs. an AI reading it. AI
might catch 90% of cases, but a 10% miss rate is
catastrophic in medicine.

Korinek & Lockwood assume AI displacement is large
and certain. But if AI prediction quality remains limited
in all these complex domains (medicine, law, education)
then humans stay very essential in these high stakes
fields and the labor displacement will be slower than
they forecast.

THE AUTHORS' CLAIM:
"A consumption tax can replace an income tax that raises the same revenue without changing work incentives, since both
reduce purchasing power by the same amount."

OUR PUSHBACK
The timing of when you feel the tax matters psychologically

Example scenario: You get a $500 bonus at work. If your employer immediately takes $150 before handing it to you, that stings in
a way that motivates you to earn more next time. If you get the full $500 but pay $150 extra at the grocery store over the next
month you barely notice it but its the same total tax and a completely different psychological experience

A tax you feel when you earn is more motivating to work harder than one you feel when you spend.

GANS CH. 3: ROC CURVE FRAMEWORK
AI prediction is characterized by a tradeoff between
true positive rates and false positive rates. Even the best
AI models operate on this curve and they are never
perfect. Think of a spam filter where if we crank it too
high and you start blocking real emails. Dial it back and
spam gets through. You're always on a tradeoff curve

THE FISCAL CRISIS
Korinek & Lockwood's Phase 1 and Phase 2 scenarios assume broad and rapid AI labor substitution. But if AI remains a
complement rather than substitute for skilled professional judgment in many domains, the transition may be gradual enough
to keep traditional tax systems in place

WHAT THE ESSAY GETS RIGHT
The structural problem is real
The tax rate gap between labor (~30%) and capital (~15-20%) is empirically documented. Labor's share of national income has
already declined from 68% to 58% since 2000, and we can observe this happening now.
Act before the crisis, not during it
History supports proactive policy: nations that anticipated economic transitions (U.S. education investment, South Korea's
knowledge economy pivot) worked better than those that reacted. Waiting for a fiscal emergency guarantees worse outcomes.
Tax consumption, not production inputs
The principle of taxing AI at the point of consumer delivery (not taxing GPUs, server farms, or B2B transactions) is smart. Think
of a law firm that gets an AI to draft documents. It wouldn’t be right to tax the AI as an equipment as that would be like taxing a
computer at a trading desk. Instead the consumption tax is at the point where the drafts are made and handed to the clients
which is the end user.

OUR VERDICT
Korinek & Lockwood identify a really interesting structural problem that many overlook when looking at AI
capabilities

chunks.section: But two assumptions are pretty misleading
chunks.text:
But two assumptions are pretty misleading:

1. The behavioral assumption that consumption taxes preserve work incentives equally to income taxes ignores
how salience shapes motivation.

2. The pace assumption that AI will broadly displace professional labor may be too aggressive — Gans shows that in
high-stakes domains, AI remains a complement to human judgment, not a substitute.

I think in this paper they are trying to be proactive in policy which is good but the specific design and assumptions of
this AI boom for the future should also take more into the behavioral economics side and the limits of AI prediction
Wll Roeschlein & Seth Greenland | Microeconomics of AI | April 20, 2025

chunks.section: New Data, New Distribution, Same Machine
chunks.text:
New Data, New Distribution, Same Machine
ECON 470 — Microeconomics of Artificial Intelligence
Spring 2026
1 Overview
In lecture you watched a 200-line GPT learn to generate baby names. In this lab you retrain the
same model on a completely different dataset — dinosaur genus names — and observe what
changes and what doesn’t. Along the way you will interpret the loss function, connect architectural
limits to the data, and see firsthand that the model is a pure prediction machine: change the data,
and the predictions change with it.
Groups: 2–3 students. At least one member of each group must have experience running Python
code (installing packages, editing a script, running it from the terminal or a notebook). The other
members do not need to know how to code — the questions are about interpreting what the model
does, not about programming. Only one submission per group, please.
Submit: A short writeup with your answers to the numbered questions below. Include screenshots
of your terminal output where indicated.
Time estimate: 2–3 hours, including training runs. Y ou can work on written answers while the
model trains. Start early — do not leave this for the night before.
Points: 100 total. See point values next to each question.
If you get stuck: The MicroGPT T utor can help you work through problems without giving away
the answer: https://chatgpt.com/g/g-69cbe9820bb481918c188ef086d7a2d8-microgpt-tutor . Use it
sparingly — you’ll learn more by wrestling with the questions yourself first.
2 Setup

3 Problem 1: T raining on Dinosaurs
3.1 Part A: The generated names
Look at the 20 sample names the model produces.
(1) Copy the 20 generated names into your writeup. F or each name, briefly note whether it
sounds like a plausible dinosaur name. How many of the 20 would you say are convincing?
What patterns did the model pick up (common endings, typical syllable structure, etc.)? [5
pts]
3.2 Part B: Interpreting the loss
The model prints a loss value at each training step. Y ou should see the loss start high and decrease
during training.
(2) Record the initial loss (step 1) and the final loss (last step). Convert each to a probability

using the formula from Lecture 1:
𝑃 = 𝑒−loss
Interpret each probability in plain language. What does the initial probability tell you about the
model before training? What does the final probability tell you about the model after training? [5
pts]
(3) Compare to the baby names model. In the baby names exercise, the initial loss was
approximately 3.3 and the final loss was approximately 2.37. Are your dinosaur losses higher
or lower? Why might this be the case? ( Hint: think about how many names are in each
dataset and how varied the character patterns are.) [5 pts]
2

3.3 Part C: The context window problem
The default block_size inmicrogpt.py is 16 , meaning the model can only “see” the last 16
characters when predicting the next one. The longest baby name in the original dataset is 15
characters, so this was fine.
(4) How long is the longest dinosaur name in the dataset? How many dinosaur names are longer

Did the loss improve, get worse, or stay about the same compared to the block_size =
16 run? What does this tell you about the relationship between architectural capacity and
prediction quality? [5 pts]

In lecture we saw a small example of automatic differentiation: 𝐿 = (𝑎 × 𝑏) + 𝑎 with 𝑎 = 2 ,𝑏 = 3 .
TheValue class inmicrogpt.py (lines 30–72) made this possible. In this problem you’ll make sure
you understand what it does and why the model needs it.

valuable: https://growingswe.com/blog/microgpt

a scalar (the .data attribute), but it also stores children and local gradients . Why does it
need those? [5 pts]

method computes and how it moves through the computation graph. [5 pts]

production function:

4.3 Part C: Why MicroGPT needs this
(12) Inmicrogpt.py , every model parameter (every weight in the embedding matrices, the atten-
tion matrices, etc.) is a Value object, not an ordinary number. The loss function is also a
Value . In 2–3 sentences, explain why . What does calling loss.backward() give the training
loop, and how does the training loop use it to make the model’s predictions better? [10 pts]

5 Problem 3: MicroOLS — Regression by Gradient Descent
In your econometrics and statistics courses you estimated regressions using Ordinary Least Squares
(OLS), which has a closed-form solution. In this problem you will estimate the exact same regression
using gradient descent and backpropagation — the same optimization machinery that trains GPT.
The point is to see that the substrate is identical: the Value class, the loss function, and the
optimizer don’t care whether they’re fitting a line or training a language model.
Download microols.py andoj.csv from Canvas. Place them in the same folder and run:
python3 microols.py
The script estimates a log-linear demand curve for orange juice using 28,947 weekly supermarket
scanner observations from three brands (Dominick’s, Minute Maid, and T ropicana):
ln(𝑄) = 𝑎 ⋅ ln(𝑃 ) + 𝑏mm ⋅MinuteMaid + 𝑏tr⋅T ropicana + 𝑏0
where 𝑄 is weekly unit sales, 𝑃 is price, and the brand variables are dummies (Dominick is the
store brand and is the omitted category). The slope 𝑎is the price elasticity of demand .
5.1 Part A: Read the code
Openmicrools.py and compare it side-by-side with microgpt.py .
(13) List three code blocks that are the same betweenmicrools.py andmicrogpt.py .[3 pts]
(14) List three things that are different .[3 pts]
5.2 Part B: T raining output
(15) Record the final values of the four parameters: a(the elasticity), b_mm (Minute Maid dummy),
b_tr (T ropicana dummy), and b0 (the intercept). Is demand elastic or inelastic? What does
the sign of atell you? What do the brand coeﬀicients tell you about how sales differ across
brands, holding price constant? [5 pts]
(16) Compare to the closed-form OLS solution. If you run OLS on this data using the
standard formula (in Excel, R, Python, or any software you like), you get 𝑎 = −3.1387 ,
𝑏mm = 0.8702 ,𝑏tr= 1.5299 , and 𝑏0= 10.8288 . How close did gradient descent get? What
does it tell you that the same algorithm that trains GPT recovers the same answer as the
closed-form formula you learned in econometrics? [5 pts]
5

5.3 Part C: Inference — sampling from the conditional distribution
After training, the script samples 20 draws from the conditional distribution of 𝑄 ∣ 𝑃 = $3.00 ,
brand = T ropicana. Each draw computes ̂ 𝑦 = 𝑎 ⋅ ln(3.00) + 𝑏tr+ 𝑏0, adds random noise from
𝑁 (0, 𝑠2
𝑒)(where 𝑠𝑒is the residual standard error), and converts back to units via 𝑄 = 𝑒ln(𝑄).
(17) Look at the 20 sampled values of 𝑄. What is the range (smallest to largest)? Does the
variation surprise you? [5 pts]
(18) Inmicrogpt.py , inference samples from a probability distribution over the next character.
Inmicrools.py , inference samples from a probability distribution over sales given price and
brand. In one or two sentences, explain what is analogous and what is different about these
two forms of “prediction. ” [3 pts]
5.4 Part D: Connecting to the course
(19) Synthesis. Y ou have now seen the same optimization engine — Value +backward() +
Adam — used to train a language model (microgpt) and to estimate a demand curve (mi-
crools). In a short paragraph, reflect on what this tells you about the claim from Gans
Chapter 1 that AI is, economically , a prediction technology . What does “prediction” mean in

to capital, tax revenue falls. Why does this happen under the current U.S. tax system?
2. The authors propose a two-phase framework. What distinguishes Phase 1 (“the twilight of
labor”) from Phase 2 (“AI-dominated economy”)?
3. The authors argue for taxing AI services to consumers rather than taxing AI equipment . What
is their reasoning?
4. The essay discusses “fixed-factor taxation” (taxing land, spectrum rights, orbital slots). Why
do the authors consider these especially attractive tax bases in an AI economy?
5. Pick one proposal from the essay — either a tax reform or a structural alternative like public
co-investment — that you found either compelling or questionable. State it in one sentence,
then say why you reacted the way you did.

1 Setup
An agent faces two equally likely states, 𝜃 ∈ {1, 2} , with Pr (𝜃 = 1) = Pr (𝜃 = 2) =1

• Safe action (𝑎 = 1 ): pays 𝑆regardless of the state.
• Risky action (𝑎 = 2 ): pays either 𝑅 or 𝑟in each state, where 𝑅 > 𝑆 > 𝑟 . The agent does
not know which.
The agent does not know what the risky action pays in either state. Judgment is the process
of experimenting — testing, piloting, researching — to find out. Each state requires its own
experiment: learning what the risky action pays in state 1 (e.g., running a clinical trial for condition
1) tells you nothing about what it pays in state 2 (condition 2 requires its own trial). The probability
that an experiment in a given state reveals a payoff of 𝑅 is𝑣, independently across states.
This gives three possible experimental outcomes when both states are investigated:
• Both states pay 𝑅: probability 𝑣2.
• One state pays 𝑅, the other 𝑟: probability 2𝑣(1 − 𝑣) .
• Both states pay 𝑟: probability (1 − 𝑣)2.

7 What This Model Cannot Show
The experimentation model establishes that prediction and judgment are complements. But it

cannot answer three important questions:
1. How does the return to prediction vary with the quality of experimentation?
There is no quality parameter — experimentation either works or is absent.
2. Is there a threshold below which experimentation is not worthwhile? No — exper-
imentation is always worth having ( 𝑉𝐽> 𝑉∅).
3. Can we get the stronger result that neither alone has value? No — experimentation
alone gives 𝑉𝐽= 77.25 > 75 = 𝑉∅.
The next part introduces a two-period model where experimentation is delayed and may fail. This
delivers all three results.
Part II: Delayed and Uncertain Experimentation (T wo-Period
Model)
W e now relax both idealizations. Experimentation is delayed by one period (discount factor 𝛿 < 1 )
and is no longer guaranteed to work: it succeeds with probability 𝜆 ∈ (0, 1) . If it succeeds, the
agent learns the full payoff function (what the risky action pays in each state). If it fails, the agent
learns nothing. The agent has one attempt.
The parameter 𝜆measures the quality of experimentation — how likely a single attempt is to
resolve the agent’s uncertainty about payoffs. Think of it as: how good is the agent’s lab, testing
process, or pilot program?
Additional parameters: 𝛿 = 0.95 .

Proof.
Parts 1 and 2. Established in Cases 1–2 (prediction alone), Case 5 (experimentation alone), and
Case 6 (both together).
Part 3. When 𝜆 > ̄𝜆,𝑉𝑃 𝐽 = 𝛿[𝑆 + 𝜆𝑣(𝑅 − 𝑆)] . Differentiating:𝜕𝑉𝑃 𝐽
𝜕𝜆= 𝛿𝑣(𝑅 − 𝑆) > 0 since 𝑅 > 𝑆
and 𝛿, 𝑣 > 0 . Better experimentation (higher 𝜆) increases the return to having a prediction.
Part 4. The marginal value of prediction without experimentation is 𝑉𝑃− 𝑉∅= 0 . The marginal
value of prediction with experimentation is 𝑉𝑃 𝐽 − 𝑉𝐽> 0 when 𝜆 > ̄𝜆. Since a positive number
exceeds zero, prediction is strictly more valuable when experimentation is available. □
The mechanism. Without prediction, experimentation reveals the full payoff function but the
agent’s action changes only when the risky action dominates in al l states (probability 𝑣2). In the
mixed case — one state pays 𝑅, the other 𝑟— the agent knows the payoffs but cannot target
the risky action to the 𝑅-state without knowing 𝜃. Going risky in both states is suboptimal by
A2, so the agent takes safe and the experimental results do not change the action. Prediction
resolves this: by revealing 𝜃, it lets the agent go risky in exactly the state that pays 𝑅. Every
𝑅-discovery (probability 𝑣) becomes action-changing, not just the both- 𝑅 coincidence (probability
𝑣2). Prediction doesn’t change what the agent learns; it changes how much of what they learn
changes their action .

In Gans’s model, the agent experiments on states one at a time and has unlimited attempts, so
experimentation alone can be valuable — the agent may learn enough to act even without knowing
the state. Prediction helps by economizing on experimentation: the agent only needs to investigate
one state instead of two.
In the two-period model, the agent learns the full payoff function in one shot but has only one
chance. Without prediction, the mixed-case results (one state pays 𝑅, the other 𝑟) do not change
the agent’s action — going risky in both states is suboptimal by A2, so the agent takes safe
regardless. Prediction changes this: knowing 𝜃lets the agent go risky in exactly the 𝑅-state,
turning experimental results that were previously inert into results that change behavior.
The core insight is identical: prediction tells you where you are; experimentation tells you
what to do there. Neither is useful alone. T ogether, they unlock decisions that neither
could support on its own.
